## reference · polished depth shadows elevation layering for mockups and mascots

* Use shadows to create depth, establish visual hierarchy, and indicate interactivity.
* Subtle shadows (lower opacity and higher blur values) tend to look more elegant and polished.
* Maintain consistency in shadow direction, X, Y, blur, and spread values.
* Match the shadow color to the surface: use darker grays or blacks on light surfaces, and a darker tint of the surface color on colored or dark backgrounds.
* Use shadows to signal interactivity, such as on clickable elements.
* Leverage tools and plugins, such as Beautiful Shadows, Shadow Maker, and SmoothShadow, to create layered, realistic shadows.
* Avoid overusing or misapplying shadows, ignoring surface context, and skipping hierarchy.
* When using drop shadows in Figma, control the position and appearance through X and Y axes, blur, and spread properties.
* To achieve a smoother drop shadow on a white surface, use a darker hue for the surface color, increase Y and blur values, and lower opacity.
* On a dark or colored surface, use a lighter color of the target component to create a glow effect, or a darker hue of the color to blend in with the background.

Sources: https://blog.logrocket.com/ux-design/shadows-ui-design-tips-best-practices/
